title: Million PDFs: Building a Modern Document Infrastructure with Rust and Typst
gist: A serverless Rust + Typst PDF-rendering architecture replaces legacy engines (Crystal Reports, LaTeX, Puppeteer) used in regulated banking and manufacturing, cutting render latency to sub-2ms with caching, memory footprint under 50MB, and cost of ~$0.50 per million PDFs — then extends the idea with Docker/Git-style content-addressable template registries to solve compliance, versioning, and reproducibility gaps that pure rendering speed doesn't address.

themes.theme: Legacy PDF tooling is an operational liability in regulated industries

insights.claim: At a bank client still running COBOL, the PDF-generation pipeline for stock/trade confirmations became so slow that customers waited days for documents, prompting a formal intervention from the German Regulatory Institute forcing a migration; two years later colleagues still had not shipped a working replacement pipeline built on AWS Lambda and Java.
insights.speaker: Erik Steiger
insights.so_what: Illustrates that legacy PDF/reporting infrastructure in banks is a multi-year, regulator-triggered migration risk — a durable pain point for vendors selling document-infrastructure modernization into financial services.
insights.quote: At some point the German Regulatory Institute was like, guys, you cannot do this. They were forced to change.

insights.claim: In a manufacturing regulatory workflow (weighing slips, medical gas certificates), a single failed certificate print required the vendor's support team to tunnel through two VPNs, download a 5-7GB production database backup into a test environment, fabricate a valid order, and manually reproduce the failure — a debugging process measured in hours per incident.
insights.speaker: Erik Steiger
insights.so_what: Quantifies the operational cost of poor observability/reproducibility in legacy document systems; content-addressable, data-linked rendering (described later in the talk) directly targets this cost center.
insights.locator: Two Experiences - Banking and Manufacturing, weighing slip anecdote

insights.claim: Crystal Reports, a document-generation tool originating in 1984, requires manual drag-and-drop field mapping per template and is Windows-only (not even supported on ARM), making multi-country template translation (e.g., adding a new factory in Czech Republic) a manual, error-prone, one-field-at-a-time process.
insights.speaker: Erik Steiger
insights.so_what: Any vendor still dependent on Crystal Reports-style tooling for compliance documents carries platform-risk (Windows/ARM incompatibility) and translation/localization cost that scales linearly with template count — a red flag in due diligence on legacy enterprise software estates.
insights.quote: This means you click on everything you see, you click on everything, copy, paste it into DPL, or your LLM and replace it. This was horrible.

themes.theme: Typst as a performance and DX upgrade over LaTeX/Puppeteer

insights.claim: Typst, a Berlin-team typesetter that emerged from a master's thesis, is a lean alternative to LaTeX: it installs in under 50MB versus LaTeX's roughly 5GB distribution, compiles faster on large files, and produces clearer error messages, while using a Markdown-like DSL syntax.
insights.speaker: Erik Steiger
insights.so_what: A concrete open-source tooling substitution investors can flag when assessing document-generation infra costs — dependency size and DX directly affect CI/CD image size, cold-start latency, and engineering velocity.

insights.claim: LaTeX rendering historically took roughly 300-500 milliseconds in the author's experience, while Puppeteer-based (headless Chrome) PDF rendering requires spinning up a full browser instance, incurring at least a full second of cold-start latency on platforms like AWS Lambda.
insights.speaker: Erik Steiger
insights.so_what: Quantified baseline for comparing document-rendering engine choices; the Puppeteer cold-start cost is a specific, avoidable tax that serverless PDF-generation vendors should be judged against.
insights.quote: If you try to use something like Puppeteer on AWS Lambda, it's going to be quite slow because you need at least a second to start the browser.

themes.theme: Serverless Rust architecture and measured performance

insights.claim: A serverless Rust architecture (two AWS Lambda functions bridged by SQS, using cargo-lambda to build Typst into a Rust Lambda) achieves rendering under 100 milliseconds, memory consumption below 50MB, and can render a million PDFs for less than 50 cents — roughly 20x cheaper than traditional (Puppeteer/LaTeX/Crystal Reports-style) approaches.
insights.speaker: Erik Steiger
insights.so_what: A concrete, benchmarkable cost/performance target (~$0.50/million PDFs, <100ms, <50MB) that any competing document-infrastructure vendor's pricing and latency claims can be measured against.
insights.quote: It uses way less memory than traditional approaches... It's below 50 megabytes, and the rendering is below 100 milliseconds... it only costs less than 50 cents to render a million PDFs. In comparison, that's like 20 times less than with other approaches.

insights.claim: With template caching, where Typst reuses the compiled layout and only recomputes the changed variable region, rendering latency on a small AWS Lambda instance drops below 2 milliseconds because most of the work is already cached.
insights.speaker: Erik Steiger
insights.so_what: The headline '<2ms' performance claim in the title is specifically a caching-driven result, not the baseline rendering speed — important nuance for anyone benchmarking or replicating the claim.
insights.quote: This makes it so fast that on a very small AWS Lambda, you can get a rendering below 2 milliseconds because most of it is cached.

insights.claim: Provisioned concurrency on AWS Lambda generates billing continuously even when idle — a cost pitfall the author discovered only after public feedback (Reddit) on his side project.
insights.speaker: Erik Steiger
insights.so_what: A specific FinOps gotcha for any serverless architecture using provisioned concurrency for latency-sensitive workloads — relevant cost-control checklist item.
insights.quote: I didn't know that if you have provisioned concurrency, it generates billing the whole time.
insights.locator: paragraph on lessons from Reddit feedback

insights.claim: Sequential (one-after-another) uploads to S3 in the rendering pipeline were identified as an unoptimized bottleneck that could reduce end-to-end latency by roughly 30% if parallelized.
insights.speaker: Erik Steiger
insights.so_what: A concrete, unimplemented optimization (parallel S3 uploads, ~30% latency reduction) — a specific technical lever for teams building similar pipelines.

themes.theme: Content-addressable template registry as the real compliance solution

insights.claim: Raw rendering speed alone doesn't solve enterprise document management: gaps remained in version control for templates, multi-file/asset support (logos, includes), on-prem deployment options for banks unwilling to use AWS, and debugging — motivating a Docker Hub/package-manager-inspired template registry design.
insights.speaker: Erik Steiger
insights.so_what: Frames the actual enterprise-sellable product as the registry/governance layer, not the renderer — rendering engines are commoditizing (open source, fast, cheap) while compliant template management remains the defensible layer.
insights.quote: Creating a Git repository for every template you have is definitely not recommended from my side.

insights.claim: The proposed template registry uses content-addressable storage (hash-based filenames providing automatic deduplication, e.g., shared logos across templates are stored once) and a Merkle-tree-style manifest structure analogous to Git and cryptocurrency data structures, with human-readable references (e.g., 'invoice:latest' or 'invoice:v3') pointing to specific content hashes.
insights.speaker: Erik Steiger
insights.so_what: A reusable architectural pattern (borrow Docker/Git primitives — content-addressing, manifests, tagged references — for non-code compliance artifacts) applicable beyond PDFs to any regulated-document workflow.
insights.locator: A New Architecture / content-addressable storage description

insights.claim: Because both the template and the input data used for each render are stored and hashed, failed renders become fully reproducible: support can look up the exact template reference and data hash tied to a customer's failed order and re-run it locally to debug, eliminating the need to pull full production database backups.
insights.speaker: Erik Steiger
insights.so_what: Directly eliminates the multi-hour VPN/backup-download debugging workflow described earlier — a quantifiable support-cost reduction pitch for regulated-industry document tooling.
insights.quote: This means we can reproduce failed renders, like take the data that was used, download it, inspect it, test it, and test it so long until you get it right.

insights.claim: The full open-source project is split into three Rust crates: a core library wrapping Typst, a registry crate handling content-addressable manifests/hashing, and a server crate exposing it all as an API — all released with no proprietary licensing.
insights.speaker: Erik Steiger
insights.so_what: Because the entire stack is open source with no license fees, competitive moats in this space will come from managed-service/compliance features, not the core rendering IP — relevant for assessing any startup building on this exact stack.
insights.quote: Everything is open source... It's split into three crates, the main library that builds around Typst.

insights.claim: The 1-million-PDF benchmark used a one-page trade-confirmation template with text, lines, vector graphics, and one PNG image, with tables that sometimes re-laid-out to two pages depending on randomized data length — a moderate-complexity, not maximally stressed, test case.
insights.speaker: Erik Steiger
insights.so_what: Caveats the headline performance numbers: results may not generalize to documents with heavy image/vector graphic density (e.g., posters with many images and complex fonts), per audience pushback in Q&A.

insights.claim: An audience member working in Java Enterprise PDF processing independently reports achieving close to 100 milliseconds per rendering using browser-based caching approaches, but notes single-digit-millisecond performance remains out of reach for their large, disclaimer-heavy documents — corroborating that caching-driven sub-10ms rendering (as claimed for Typst) depends heavily on document complexity.
insights.speaker: Erik Steiger
insights.so_what: Independent practitioner validation that sub-10ms rendering is achievable only for certain document profiles — tempers the generalizability of the headline Typst benchmark.
insights.quote: Depending on how complicated it is, with caching, you get sub-10 millisecond.

insights.claim: Because the content-addressable manifest hash changes with any difference in the underlying data or template (even invisible ones), the registry cannot support fuzzy/approximate matching to find 'the closest' template used for a given rendered PDF — only exact reverse-lookup when the data was stored digitally.
insights.speaker: Erik Steiger
insights.so_what: A known limitation for reverse-engineering/reconciliation use cases (e.g., matching legacy PDFs back to templates) — relevant for any buyer expecting fuzzy-match capability from this architecture.
insights.quote: If it's visually just a little bit different, even if there's just something different that's not visible in the PDF, this would change the hash.
